conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, let me read the official guidance to ensure I provide you with accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 17 years old and lives with me here in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have one child, Alex, who is 17 years old and lives with you in Manchester.

Since Alex is 17 (over 16), I need to ask: **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

(Approved education or training typically includes things like A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3, and other similar qualifications.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any approved education or training. He finished school and decided not to continue with A-levels or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a follow-up question:

**Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services after leaving education?**

(This is relevant because Child Benefit can continue for 20 weeks after a 16 or 17 year old leaves education if they register with one of these services.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with the careers service or the armed forces. He just left school and started working.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to ask one more important question:

**How many hours per week does Alex work in his paid job?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He works full-time - it's about 24 hours a week or more. He's got a proper job at the local supermarket.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me summarize what you've told me:
- You have one child, Alex, who is 17 years old
- Alex lives with you in Manchester
- Alex is not in approved education or training
- Alex has not registered with a careers service or armed services
- Alex works 24 hours or more per week in paid employment

Based on the official UK government guidance, I can now provide you with a determination.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Assessment Result: Not Eligible**

I'm sorry to inform you that you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why:

While Child Benefit normally continues for children aged 16-19 if they stay in approved education or training, Alex is not in approved education or training. According to the official rules, Child Benefit stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training.

Since Alex works 24 hours or more per week at the supermarket and is not in approved education or training, your entitlement to Child Benefit for him has ended.

**What this means:** If you were previously receiving Child Benefit for Alex, you should inform HMRC that his circumstances have changed, as your entitlement will have ceased when he started working 24+ hours per week.

I hope this clarifies your situation. If Alex's circumstances change in the future (for example, if he decides to return to approved education or training and reduces his working hours), you may be able to claim again.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old, which means he is over 16. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you can continue to claim Child Benefit for a child aged 16-19 if they stay in approved education or training. However, Alex is not in approved education or training. Additionally, the rules state that you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex works 24 hours or more per week at the local supermarket and is not in approved education or training, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

According to the rules, Child Benefit stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
